question: What evidence supports preemptive TEVAR for uncomplicated acute or subacute type B aortic dissection?
source_access_type: Original publisher PDF held in private local source workspace
source_version_or_date: Journal of Vascular Surgery. 2015;61(5):1192-1199. Submitted October 16, 2014; accepted December 8, 2014

study_characteristics.design: Retrospective, single-center observational cohort assembled from a centralized clinical registry and chart review
study_characteristics.population: 298 patients presenting to Massachusetts General Hospital within 14 days of symptoms with imaging-confirmed type B aortic dissection and an initial plan for medical management alone
study_characteristics.setting: Massachusetts General Hospital, March 1999 through March 2011
study_characteristics.exclusions: Type A dissection, intramural hematoma, penetrating aortic ulcer, rupture, or immediate malperfusion requiring operative intervention
study_characteristics.exposure: Initial medical management; patients were subsequently classified by whether an aorta-related intervention occurred
study_characteristics.comparator: Post hoc comparison of patients who eventually underwent intervention versus those remaining medically managed; not randomized and not a baseline treatment comparison
study_characteristics.primary_outcomes: Survival, freedom from intervention, and intervention-free survival
study_characteristics.medical_failure_definition: Death from any cause or a complication of dissection requiring intervention; early failure occurred within 15 days of starting medical therapy
study_characteristics.follow_up: Outcomes collected through December 2013; mean follow-up reported as 4.3 ± 3.5 years in the Results (4.2 years, range 0.1-14.7 years in the Abstract)
study_characteristics.analysis: Kaplan-Meier life tables and backward stepwise regression of demographic and comorbidity variables
study_characteristics.source_location: Printed pp. 1192-1194; PDF pp. 1-3; Abstract, Methods, Results, Tables I-III

primary_fields.pmid: Registered in catalog; not printed in supplied PDF

source_identity_validation.metadata_warning: The publisher PDF identifies Journal of Vascular Surgery 61 (2015) 1192-1199 and DOI 10.1016/j.jvs.2014.12.038. PMID 25659458 is registered in the catalog but is not printed in the supplied PDF. No conflicting identifier was found.

extracted_claims.claim_text: This was a retrospective single-center cohort of patients with acute type B aortic dissection who were initially managed medically.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Partners HealthCare maintains a centralized clinical data registry of all inpatient and outpatient encounters within the system. This database, the Research Patient Data Registry, was searched for the International Classification of Diseases, Ninth Revision, code for patients with aortic dissection who presented to the Massachusetts General Hospital between March 1999 and March 2011. Patient charts were reviewed
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The retrospective, single-institution design is also identified as a limitation on printed p. 1197 / PDF p. 6.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The cohort defined acute presentation as within 14 days of symptoms and excluded rupture or immediate malperfusion requiring intervention.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: those who presented within 14 days of the development of symptoms of the acute aortic syndrome and had axial imaging that confirmed an aortic dissection wherein the entry tear was distal to the left subclavian artery were considered to have an acute type B aortic dissection and included in the patient cohort. ... Patients with type A dissection were excluded from the study, as were those patients who presented with rupture or immediate signs of malperfusion mandating operative intervention.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Intramural hematomas and penetrating aortic ulcers were also excluded. The paper describes the resulting cohort as uncomplicated, but some initially uncomplicated patients developed early complications after enrollment.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Medical-treatment failure was a composite of all-cause death or a dissection complication requiring intervention, with early failure defined within 15 days.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Failure of medical therapy was defined as either death or a complication of dissection requiring intervention. Early failures occurred within 15 days of initiation of medical therapy.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Because the death component was all-cause, this composite is not a purely aorta-related endpoint. Death and intervention could overlap within the failure count.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Among 298 initially medically managed patients, 174 met the composite failure definition during follow-up, including 87 aorta-related interventions and 119 deaths.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: During a mean follow-up of 4.3 ± 3.5 years, failure of medical therapy occurred in 174 patients (58.4%). There were 87 (29.2%) aorta-related interventions and 119 (38.3%) deaths.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The printed 38.3% for 119 deaths is arithmetically inconsistent with n=298 and is preserved as printed rather than corrected. The 174 composite failures are fewer than deaths plus interventions because categories may overlap.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; reported percentage discrepancy pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Most aorta-related interventions were open operations, and aneurysmal degeneration was the most frequent indication.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: An open operative approach was taken in 63 cases (72.4%). In 57 patients (65.5%), the indication for operation was aneurysmal degeneration.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Table III reports 63 open and 24 endovascular interventions among 87 total interventions. This cohort does not directly compare preemptive TEVAR with medical therapy.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Early failure occurred in approximately one eighth of the initially medically managed cohort, most often involving death or intervention for evolving complications.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: There were 37 (12.4%) early failures, 25 (8.4%) of which were operative interventions. ... The indication for early operation in a majority of patients was either renal ischemia (36.0%) or mesenteric ischemia (28.0%). Early aneurysmal degeneration was the indication for intervention in 24.0% of early operations
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Table II reports 37 early failures, 15 deaths, and 25 interventions; these categories overlap. The Abstract instead reports 14 early deaths, an unresolved internal discrepancy.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; early-death discrepancy pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Freedom from intervention remained about three quarters at six years, whereas intervention-free survival declined to 41%.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: The actuarial freedom from intervention was 77.3% ± 2.4% after 3 years and 74.2% ± 2.5% after 6 years. ... Intervention-free survival was 55.0% ± 3.0% after 3 years and 41.0% ± 3.2% after 6 years.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Intervention-free survival combines intervention and all-cause death, so the 41% estimate does not mean that 59% required an aortic intervention.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: End-stage renal disease was associated with the composite failure endpoint, while the reported age association was small and potentially reflected selection bias.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: End-stage renal disease was predictive of medical failure (hazard ratio, 2.60; confidence interval, 1.19-5.66; P = .02), whereas age >70 years was protective (hazard ratio, 0.97; confidence interval, 0.95-0.98; P < .01)
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Table IV prints P=.03 for age >70, whereas the Results and Abstract print P<.01. In the published discussion on printed p. 1199, the author states that the age finding may highlight selection bias and may have limited clinical significance.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; P-value discrepancy and clinical interpretation pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Six-year survival was higher among patients who eventually underwent intervention, but this post hoc observational comparison does not establish that intervention caused better survival.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Although survival from the time of presentation between the two groups was similar at 3 years, at 6 years, survival was significantly better in the patients who underwent operative intervention (76.4% ± 4.7%) than in the medically treated cohort (59.3% ± 3.8%; P < .05)
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Figure 3 prints P=.018. Group membership depended on a future intervention, creating major selection and survivor/immortal-time bias. There was no baseline randomization, time-dependent exposure model, or reported adjustment for this comparison. The Abstract rounds the estimates to 76% versus 58%.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; causal interpretation specifically unsupported

extracted_claims.claim_text: The study could not identify anatomical predictors of failure and called for further study to determine which patients might benefit from early intervention.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Another limitation of the study was lack of reliable data to determine anatomic variables. ... further study is needed to determine which patients presenting with acute type B dissection will benefit from early intervention.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: In the published discussion on printed p. 1198 / PDF p. 7, the author confirms that only demographic and comorbidity risk factors were analyzed and that anatomical correlates required future study.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Cause-specific mortality was unavailable, and the retrospective single-center design introduced treatment and selection bias.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: One of the limitations of this study was the use of all-cause mortality in determining our primary end point. Certainly, aorta-specific mortality would have provided a more thorough analysis on the natural history of medically managed acute type B aortic dissection. However, these data were simply not available to us as a significant number of patients died outside of our hospital system. ... The retrospective nature of the database and the treatment biases that exist from reviewing the records of only a single institution are also noted limitations.
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The published discussion further identifies possible age-related intervention selection and notes that postoperative complications other than 30-day mortality were not analyzed.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

outcome_data.outcome_name: Composite failure of initial medical therapy
outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.result_text: 174 patients (58.4%) experienced all-cause death or an aorta-related intervention

outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.intervention: Initial medical management with possible subsequent intervention
outcome_data.reviewer_note: The printed percentage is arithmetically inconsistent with 119/298 and requires specialist/source-author clarification; it has not been recalculated in the evidence record.

outcome_data.intervention: Aortic intervention after initial medical management
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed p. 1194; PDF p. 3; Results; Table III

outcome_data.population: 87 patients undergoing intervention after initial medical management
outcome_data.follow_up: Mean time to operation 2.3 years in this subset
outcome_data.result_text: 57 of 87 interventions (65.5%) were for aneurysmal degeneration
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed p. 1194; PDF p. 3; Results; Table III

outcome_data.outcome_name: Early failure of initial medical therapy
outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.follow_up: Within 15 days of starting medical therapy
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed p. 1194; PDF p. 3; Results; Tables II and III

outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.intervention: Initial medical management with possible subsequent intervention
outcome_data.result_text: Table II reports 15 deaths (5.0%); the Abstract reports 14 deaths
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed pp. 1192 and 1194; PDF pp. 1 and 3; Abstract; Table II
outcome_data.reviewer_note: Unresolved internal discrepancy between Abstract and Table II.

outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed pp. 1194-1195; PDF pp. 3-4; Results; Figure 1

outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed pp. 1194-1195; PDF pp. 3-4; Results; Figure 2
outcome_data.reviewer_note: Composite of intervention and all-cause death; not the intervention rate alone.

outcome_data.outcome_name: End-stage renal disease association with medical failure
outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.intervention: Not an intervention comparison; backward stepwise regression
outcome_data.comparator: End-stage renal disease present versus absent
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed pp. 1194-1196; PDF pp. 3-5; Results; Table IV

outcome_data.outcome_name: Age over 70 association with medical failure
outcome_data.population: 298 initially medically managed acute type B aortic dissection patients
outcome_data.intervention: Not an intervention comparison; backward stepwise regression
outcome_data.comparator: Age over 70 versus other ages as modeled
outcome_data.result_text: Hazard ratio 0.97, 95% confidence interval 0.95-0.98; P<.01 in text and P=.03 in Table IV
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed pp. 1194 and 1196; PDF pp. 3 and 5; Results; Table IV
outcome_data.reviewer_note: Internal P-value discrepancy. Published discussion suggests possible intervention-selection bias and questions clinical significance.

outcome_data.outcome_name: Six-year survival by eventual intervention status
outcome_data.population: Patients classified post hoc by whether they eventually underwent intervention
outcome_data.intervention: Eventual aortic intervention after initial medical management
outcome_data.page_or_location: Printed pp. 1194 and 1197; PDF pp. 3 and 6; Results; Figure 3
outcome_data.reviewer_note: Post hoc, nonrandomized grouping by a future intervention is subject to selection and survivor/immortal-time bias; this outcome cannot establish causal benefit.

limitations: Retrospective, single-center observational design with treatment-selection bias and limited generalizability.
There was no randomized or baseline comparator of early TEVAR versus continued medical therapy. Most interventions were open, and interventions occurred for heterogeneous evolving indications.

Classifying patients by an intervention that occurred during follow-up and measuring survival from presentation introduces survivor/immortal-time bias; no time-dependent exposure analysis was reported.

The acute cohort excluded rupture and immediate malperfusion, but some patients developed early malperfusion or other complications. Findings should not be applied to initially complicated TBAD or treated as purely prophylactic-intervention evidence.
Medical failure combined all-cause death and aorta-related intervention. Cause-specific mortality was unavailable for many deaths outside the hospital system.
Reliable anatomical variables were unavailable, so proposed high-risk imaging features and remodeling predictors were not evaluated.
Only demographic and comorbidity variables were entered into backward stepwise regression; unmeasured and residual confounding remain substantial.

The study reports internal numerical inconsistencies: 14 early deaths in the Abstract versus 15 in Table II; P<.01 for age in the text versus P=.03 in Table IV; and 119 deaths printed as 38.3% despite a cohort of 298.
Follow-up imaging followed institutional practice, but completeness of serial imaging and missing-data handling were not reported in detail.
Postoperative complications other than 30-day mortality were not analyzed, limiting assessment of the harms of intervention.
The paper's discussion of a potential survival advantage after intervention is associative and must remain distinct from randomized evidence.
contradictions: The Abstract reports 14 early deaths, whereas Table II reports 15 early deaths (5.0%).
The text and Abstract report P<.01 for the age-over-70 association, whereas Table IV reports P=.03.
The Results print 119 deaths as 38.3%; that percentage is not arithmetically consistent with the stated cohort denominator of 298.
The authors describe better survival in patients who underwent intervention, but the nonrandomized post hoc grouping and survivor/immortal-time bias prevent a causal interpretation.
reviewer_notes: Original-PDF extraction only. This record describes retrospective observational natural-history evidence, not a randomized comparison of preemptive TEVAR versus medical therapy. All claims and outcomes remain Pending; none is reviewer verified or suitable for a generated answer. Internal numerical discrepancies are preserved and flagged rather than silently corrected.
